title: Invisible Men: Mass Incarceration and the Myth of Black Progress By Becky Pettit Russell Sage Foundation. 2012. 141 pages. $29.95 paper
abstract: The fivefold increase in the American imprisonment rate since the mid-1970s seems to coincide with an equivalent increase in the number of books on this phenomenon—as well as names for it. As this subfield is so new and the number of books in it so vast, it makes deciding on what is the canon in this area difficult. Based on my reading of Becky Pettit's Invisible Men, I would place it squarely in the canon for this subfield, which to me means that it is (1. one of the first five books I would ask anyone wanting to get into this subfield to read before doing so, (2. a book I would use in all my upper-level undergraduate courses and graduate courses, and (3. one of the tiny handful of books to draw inspiration from whenever I consider starting a new project. This is a strong claim, but it is merited for two reasons. First, this work greatly extends the “invisibility” argument advanced in earlier work in this area. Previous research on how mass imprisonment distorts social statistics focused on the labor market, demonstrating that high rates of incarceration make the American labor market seem exceptionally healthy relative to other developed democracies, which it is not once our millions of inmates are considered out of the labor force or their (lack of) wages are counted (e.g., Western and Beckett 1999; Western 2006).
